items.prompt_md: Why does XOR-ing every element of the array together isolate the single non-duplicated value?
options: Because XOR is only defined for two operands, so pairs always cancel
Because `x ^ x = 0` and `x ^ 0 = x`, so every duplicate pair cancels to zero and only the lone value survives
Because XOR sorts values in ascending order as it combines them
Because XOR is equivalent to summing the array modulo 2
items.answer: Because `x ^ x = 0` and `x ^ 0 = x`, so every duplicate pair cancels to zero and only the lone value survives
items.explanation_md: XOR is commutative and associative, so regardless of order every value that appears twice cancels itself out (`x ^ x = 0`), leaving only the unpaired value XORed with 0 — itself.

items.prompt_md: Why does the order in which you XOR the array elements not matter?
options: XOR is commutative and associative, so any grouping or ordering of the operands produces the same result
The array must be sorted first for XOR to work correctly
XOR only depends on the last element processed
Order matters, but the effect is negligible for small arrays
items.answer: XOR is commutative and associative, so any grouping or ordering of the operands produces the same result
items.explanation_md: Because `a ^ b == b ^ a` and `(a ^ b) ^ c == a ^ (b ^ c)`, you can fold the array in any order — the duplicate pairs still find each other and cancel.

items.prompt_md: You need to find the one number missing from the range `0..n` inside an array of `n` values (each other value present exactly once). How can the XOR trick be adapted?
options: XOR all the array values together with all the integers `0..n`; every present value cancels with its counterpart, leaving the missing one
XOR only the array values together — the missing number falls out automatically
Sum the array and subtract from `n`; XOR cannot handle missing values
XOR is not applicable to missing-number problems, only duplicate problems
items.answer: XOR all the array values together with all the integers `0..n`; every present value cancels with its counterpart, leaving the missing one
items.explanation_md: Folding in the full expected range `0..n` alongside the array turns 'missing value' into the same cancellation shape as 'duplicate value' — everything pairs off except the one number that was never present.

items.prompt_md: Which identity lets you swap two integer variables `a` and `b` without a temporary variable, using XOR?
options: `a = b; b = a;`
`a += b; b = a - b; a -= b;` combined with XOR
items.explanation_md: Each step folds one variable's bits into the other via XOR; because XOR is its own inverse, three folds recover both original values swapped, with no third slot needed.

items.prompt_md: What is the XOR of all integers from 1 to 4, computed step by step?
items.explanation_md: `1^2=3`, `3^3=0`, `0^4=4` — walking the fold left to right shows the intermediate cancellation, ending at 4.

items.prompt_md: A problem says every value appears exactly **three** times except one, which appears once. Why does the plain `result ^= x` single-accumulator trick fail here?
options: XOR-ing a value with itself three times leaves the value itself, not zero, so triples don't cancel the way pairs do
XOR only works on arrays of even length
The accumulator overflows after three XORs
It still works identically, no changes needed
items.answer: XOR-ing a value with itself three times leaves the value itself, not zero, so triples don't cancel the way pairs do
items.explanation_md: `x ^ x ^ x == x`, not 0, because XOR toggles bits an odd number of times — triples need bit-counting per position (mod 3) instead of a plain fold.

items.prompt_md: Why is XOR described as "a value cancelling itself" the key structural insight, rather than the specific line of code?
options: Because the same cancellation idea applies to any problem where 'noise' appears in pairs and exactly one value breaks the pattern, not just this one array
Because the code line itself is difficult to memorize
Because XOR is the only bitwise operator worth learning
Because the insight only applies to integers, not other data types
items.answer: Because the same cancellation idea applies to any problem where 'noise' appears in pairs and exactly one value breaks the pattern, not just this one array
items.explanation_md: The lesson explicitly frames spotting the pairing/cancelling structure — not the one-liner — as the transferable skill, since it recurs across many differently-worded problems.

items.prompt_md: A common bug when porting XOR tricks between languages is mishandling the sign bit. Why does this matter?
options: Some languages (like Java) use fixed-width signed integers, so a XOR result with the high bit set is interpreted as negative, which can break comparisons or output expectations
XOR cannot be computed on negative numbers in any language
The sign bit is always ignored by the XOR operator
Sign bit issues only appear when XOR-ing floating point numbers
items.answer: Some languages (like Java) use fixed-width signed integers, so a XOR result with the high bit set is interpreted as negative, which can break comparisons or output expectations
items.explanation_md: XOR just toggles bits — if the top bit ends up set, a signed fixed-width integer type reads that as a negative number even though the bit pattern is 'correct', so the interpretation (not the operation) is the trap.

items.prompt_md: Which operator-precedence mistake is a classic bug when writing `if x ^ mask == 0:` in a language like Python?
options: `==` binds tighter than `^`, so the expression actually evaluates as `x ^ (mask == 0)` instead of `(x ^ mask) == 0`
`^` binds tighter than `==`, so parentheses are never needed
Python does not support the `^` operator on integers
The mistake only affects floating point comparisons
items.answer: `==` binds tighter than `^`, so the expression actually evaluates as `x ^ (mask == 0)` instead of `(x ^ mask) == 0`
items.explanation_md: In Python (and several C-family languages), comparison operators bind tighter than bitwise XOR, so an un-parenthesized `x ^ mask == 0` silently computes something other than the intended equality check on the XOR result.

items.prompt_md: What is the time and space complexity of the one-pass XOR solution to find the single number?
options: O(n) time, O(1) extra space
O(n) time, O(n) extra space
O(n log n) time, O(1) extra space
O(n^2) time, O(1) extra space
items.answer: O(n) time, O(1) extra space
items.explanation_md: One pass over the array, one XOR per element, and a single integer accumulator — O(n) time and O(1) extra space, no map or sort required.

items.prompt_md: What is the time and space complexity of the hash-map frequency-count approach to the same 'find the single value' problem?
options: O(n) time, O(n) extra space
O(n) time, O(1) extra space
O(n log n) time, O(n) extra space
O(1) time, O(n) extra space
items.answer: O(n) time, O(n) extra space
items.explanation_md: Building the frequency map still takes one O(n) pass, but now every distinct key is stored, so extra space grows with the number of distinct values — O(n) in the worst case.

items.prompt_md: For the XOR-with-range trick that finds a missing number in `0..n`, what is the time and space complexity?
options: O(n) time, O(1) extra space
O(n^2) time, O(1) extra space
O(n) time, O(n) extra space
O(log n) time, O(1) extra space
items.answer: O(n) time, O(1) extra space
items.explanation_md: Folding the array values and the range `0..n` together is still just one pass of XORs into a single accumulator — O(n) time, O(1) extra space, same as the plain single-number trick.

items.prompt_md: Every value in the array appears exactly twice except one value, which appears once. You need to find it in O(n) time and O(1) extra space. Which approach fits?
options: Sort the array, then scan for the position where neighbors differ
Keep a hash map counting occurrences of each value
XOR every element together — duplicate pairs cancel to zero, leaving the answer
Use two pointers converging from both ends of the array
items.answer: XOR every element together — duplicate pairs cancel to zero, leaving the answer
items.explanation_md: The O(1)-space requirement rules out the hash map, and sorting/two-pointers don't exploit the pairing structure at all. A single XOR accumulator cancels every duplicate in one pass with no extra memory.

items.prompt_md: You're given an array of `n` distinct integers taken from the range `0..n`, with exactly one number missing. You want the missing number in one linear pass with no extra array. Which approach fits?
options: Hash map of counts for every possible value in the range
XOR every array value together with every integer in `0..n` — everything present cancels, leaving the missing one
Sort the array and binary-search for the gap
Build a running sum and compare it against a second running sum
items.answer: XOR every array value together with every integer in `0..n` — everything present cancels, leaving the missing one
items.explanation_md: The pairing/cancellation trick extends naturally: folding in the full expected range means every present value meets its 'partner' and only the missing one survives, in O(1) space.

items.prompt_md: You need to swap two integer variables in a tight loop, and you're told not to introduce a third temporary variable. Which approach fits?
options: Use three chained XOR assignments — each fold packs one variable's bits into the other, unpacking them swapped
Allocate a small fixed-size array to hold both values temporarily
Use a hash map keyed by variable name
Sort the two values so the smaller one is always first
items.answer: Use three chained XOR assignments — each fold packs one variable's bits into the other, unpacking them swapped
items.explanation_md: XOR is its own inverse, so three folds between the two variables recover both values swapped without ever needing a third storage slot.

items.prompt_md: You must compute the cumulative XOR of every integer from 1 to n efficiently, without looping and folding one by one for very large n. Which approach fits?
options: Sort the range first, then fold pairs from the ends inward
Use the known 4-cycle pattern of `n XOR (n-1) XOR ... XOR 1` based on `n mod 4` to get the answer in O(1)
Keep a hash map of how many times each bit position has been toggled
Use two pointers walking from 1 and n toward the middle
items.answer: Use the known 4-cycle pattern of `n XOR (n-1) XOR ... XOR 1` based on `n mod 4` to get the answer in O(1)
items.explanation_md: The XOR of 1..n repeats in a 4-cycle (n, 1, n+1, 0 depending on n mod 4), so the whole range's XOR reduces to a constant-time formula instead of an explicit fold.

items.prompt_md: An array's values all repeat exactly twice except for **two** values, each of which appears exactly once, and you must find both in O(n) time, O(1) space. Which approach fits?
options: XOR everything together, then use a set bit in the combined XOR to split the array into two groups and XOR each group separately
XOR everything together once and treat the result as one of the two missing values
Sort the array and scan for two adjacent gaps
Build a hash map of counts and read off the two singleton keys
items.answer: XOR everything together, then use a set bit in the combined XOR to split the array into two groups and XOR each group separately
items.explanation_md: A single fold only gives `a ^ b`, not `a` and `b` individually — but any bit set in `a ^ b` must differ between them, so partitioning by that bit and folding each half in isolation recovers both values in O(1) space.

items.prompt_md: You need to check whether two integers differ in exactly one bit position (a classic 'flip one bit to get from A to B' check). Which approach fits?
options: XOR the two numbers, then confirm the result is a power of two (exactly one bit set)
Convert both numbers to strings and compare character by character
Sort the bits of each number and compare the sorted sequences
Use a hash map mapping each integer to its bit pattern
items.answer: XOR the two numbers, then confirm the result is a power of two (exactly one bit set)
items.explanation_md: XOR-ing two integers marks exactly the bit positions where they differ; if that result has only one set bit (checkable via a mask trick), the two numbers differ by a single flipped bit.

items.prompt_md: You need to know, for every value in a large array, how many times it occurred, so you can list all values that occur an odd number of times. Which approach fits?
options: Hash map of counts, then filter for odd counts
XOR everything together — the fold only ever gives you the values that occur an odd number of times combined into one number, not the individual counts
Sort the array and scan for consecutive-count parity
Bit mask popcount over the whole array treated as one bit-string
items.answer: Hash map of counts, then filter for odd counts
items.explanation_md: Once you need actual per-value counts (not just 'what's left after cancellation'), XOR throws away exactly the information you need — a hash map of counts is the right tool here, not the cancellation trick.

items.prompt_md: You must find the single number in an array where every other value appears exactly twice, but you're explicitly told extra memory proportional to the input is fine and simplicity matters more than shaving constants. Which approach still fits best?
options: A hash map counting occurrences and returning the key with count 1
Sort the array and binary search for the gap
A four-pointer boundary walk
Backtracking over all subsets of the array
items.answer: A hash map counting occurrences and returning the key with count 1
items.explanation_md: When the O(1)-space constraint is lifted, a hash map is a perfectly valid (if less elegant) fit for the same pairing structure — the XOR trick is a space optimization, not the only correct answer to the underlying question.

items.prompt_md: You need to toggle a specific feature flag stored as a single bit within an integer, turning it on if off and off if on, in O(1) time. Which approach fits?
options: XOR the integer with a mask that has only that bit set
Convert to binary string, flip the character, convert back
Use a hash map keyed by flag name
Sort the bits and swap the target position
items.answer: XOR the integer with a mask that has only that bit set
items.explanation_md: XOR-ing against a single-bit mask flips exactly that bit and leaves every other bit untouched, which is precisely a bit toggle — no string conversion or extra structure needed.

items.prompt_md: You're asked to detect duplicate rows in a large 2D table by comparing full rows for equality, where rows can have arbitrary non-numeric contents. Which approach fits best?
options: XOR every cell together — matching rows will cancel out
Use a hash set of row representations (e.g., tuples) and check membership
Sort every row's cells and compare sorted sequences pairwise
Bit-mask popcount over each row
items.answer: Use a hash set of row representations (e.g., tuples) and check membership
items.explanation_md: XOR only cancels numeric values that appear in matched pairs across the *whole* collection — it has no notion of 'row equality' for arbitrary contents, so a hash set of row keys is the correct tool here.
